data.title: AppLovin: Your Golden Buying Opportunity Ahead Of Earnings (Preview)
data.description:
Investment Rating: “Buy”

AppLovin Corporation (APP) is a software-based platform for advertisers that initially operated across Advertising and Apps but then sold the Apps business and focused entirely on developing its Advertising segment because the launch of AXON 2.0 promised to deliver higher ROI over time. The stock price made a massive surge over the past 5 years, growing from about $60/share to over $740/share at some point. As I'm writing these lines, APP is trading at $569/share after its YTD correction of ~16%.

The company is approaching its earnings release in 2 weeks (February 11th), and I believe the current sentiment of the market might abruptly change and provide a great opportunity for dip-buyers as the company beats the consensus again, guiding for a strong 2026 thanks to the continued adoption of their new offerings among clients (I'm talking about their AXON Ads Manager launched in October 2025). For the level of growth that should come in the next few years (both in terms of the sales and earnings per share), APP is priced at a discount, in my opinion. I've calculated a meaningful upside potential for the next 12-24 months, and it makes me bullish on AppLovin for the medium term.

From Niche Leader To Global Ad-Tech Challenger

AppLovin's AXON 2.0 engine runs proprietary machine learning algos, processing trillions of data points in order to make close-to-real-time predictions for a better matching of advertisers and publishers (through automated auctions). It sounds complicated, but in a nutshell, this engine helps APP stand out among other digital ad platform providers because, through the quality of its prediction system, it allows advertisers to hit specific return on ad spend (ROAS) targets with quite high precision.

AppLovin utilizes its own first-party data for AXON 2.0, so its capability of performing highly granular optimizations is something that is usually impossible to achieve for its peers, who have to use lossy third-party data. This particularity, or better called competitive advantage, helped APP basically quadruple the ad spend on its platform in just 2 years, and the growth isn't slowing down, as far as we can see it from the latest results.

I strongly believe that the level of growth that AppLovin has managed to achieve isn't going away in 2026-2027.

They launched AXON Ads Manager on October 1, 2025, so it's been only a few months since it saw the light. The early data sends us optimistic signals—the overall spend from self-service advertisers went up by 50% w/w in the early weeks. So, with more APP's customers getting used to the new platform (it takes time according to the management's commentaries), the current massive customer base of ~1 billion DAU is likely to boost the top line throughout the year. Specifically for the upcoming quarter, the management guided for 12-14% QoQ growth in revenues, so we won't have to wait for too long before the factual success of AXON 2.0 and AXON Ads Manager contributes to the revenue growth acceleration.

After their portfolio of first-party mobile games divestment, the business structure has been simplified, and the company is going to be judged as an elite enterprise software platform going forward thanks to its above-average margins.

I should note here that the current price action—the YTD correction—seems to be mainly dominated by the recently published short report:

But the management has already rejected all the capital structure issues raised, asserting full compliance with the SEC.

I think the negative sentiment is likely to change over time—that's my base expectation. Especially so if the company keeps delivering on the management's guidance and continues to expand.

Let's Preview APP's Upcoming Q4

As you could have seen above, the adjusted EBITDA margin in Q3 2025 amounted to ~82% (vs. already solid 77% in Q3 2024), and the trend remains upward-sloping in nature. I don't expect a sudden drop in margins in the upcoming Q4 report. The management guided for a range of 82-83% on the adjusted EBITDA level.

In the fourth quarter of 2025, we anticipate revenue between $1.570 billion and $1.6 billion, reflecting between 12% and 14% sequential growth. With adjusted EBITDA between $1.290 billion and $1.320 billion, targeting an adjusted EBITDA margin of 82% to 83%.

Source: APP's earnings call (Q3 2025).

In Q2, they were expecting a margin of 81% (no range) for Q3, so the factual Q3 metric beat the management's guidance by about 100 basis points. It's a very strong beat that is directly translating to EPS growth, helping APP beat the key headline figure (albeit slightly).

The key difference now lies in revisions. After the Q2 beat, analysts were aggressively bullish on the nearest quarters, lifting estimates by 10-30% in some cases. After the modest beat of less than 3% in Q3, they were less enthusiastic, lifting the consensus figures only for far-end forward years.

The quarterly revisions have been basically silent for the past month, and the consensus EPS estimates have gained only 2-3% over the past 3 months (compare it to the 6 months below).

It has become easier for APP to beat on the bottom line this time, in my view, especially if we assume the firm is able to repeat its Q2 success and reach an above-guided EBITDA margin.

AppLovin's Growth Is Worth The Price Tag

As I've already mentioned above, the growth at AppLovin isn't stopping. According to Seeking Alpha, APP has managed to reach a 5-year CAGR of over 34% in terms of revenues, and its net loss of $125.2 million turned to a net profit of over $2.8 billion (on a TTM basis). For the next 5 years, the EPS and revenues CAGRs are expected to be at 25.9% and 30.2%, respectively.

For this level of growth, the market is pricing the stock at less than 39x FY2026 earnings and less than 30x FY2027 earnings.

We have to recall here that APP is translating over 80% of its revenues into adjusted EBITDA, and most of it then transfers into FCF. These profitability levels are hard to compare with when we try to find a market peer.

I assume that APP, for the level of its superior profitability, should be valued at a PEG of 1. Even if they don't beat the FY2025 EPS at all, following the Q3 path (when they barely beat the consensus on EPS), the stock would appear cheap at that PEG ratio.

PEG = price-to-earnings / EPS growth YoY

if PEG = 1, EPS growth = 56.63%, and EPS = $14.64 (all consensus), then:

1 * $14.64 * 56.63 = $829.06/share

The current stock price is ~$569.24, so the upside potential that I've just arrived at points to 45-46% for the next 12-24 months.

Risks To My Views

My thesis is based on the core assumption that AppLovin will keep growing its revenues and EPS over the next years at least at the consensus levels. But if the actual growth in 2026-2028 turns out to be lower than what the market is pricing in—and we see that in Q3 the positive surprise was very low—the market is unlikely to give APP 40-45x in earnings multiple. Macro risks and the criticality of the ads market may contribute to that. In this case, the upside that I calculated in this article will become misleading and wrong.

Conclusion

AppLovin and its highly efficient, highly profitable business model stand out among tech peers when we compare forward growth to forward multiples. I like that the Q4 expectations are not pricing in excessive premiums, making it easier for AppLovin to beat more meaningfully compared to what we saw in Q3. The company reports its Q4 results on February 11th, in less than two weeks, and I think the dip it's going through is giving investors a nice buying opportunity.

data.title: Danaher Stock Plunge Does Not Represent A Good Entry Point
data.description:
Jan. 28 was a pretty painful day for shareholders of Danaher Corporation (DHR). Shares of the stock dropped 4.8% after management announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Although it looks as though 2026 will be positive for the company, and even though the company exceeded analysts’ estimates when it came to revenue and adjusted earnings per share, GAAP earnings per share fell slightly short of expectations. Even after this sizable decline in share price, the stock is outperforming since I called it a "Hold" back in September 2025. Units are up 15.1%, comfortably outperforming the 7.4% jump experienced by the S&P 500.

From a purely fundamental standpoint, I would say that things are going well. Revenue, profits, and cash flows are all rising nicely. Leverage for the company is in check. And relative to other similar firms, shares are actually attractively priced. But unfortunately, right now, the stock is fairly pricey on an absolute basis. That is what has prevented me in the past from upgrading it to something more bullish. And even after improved financial performance and a decline in share price that occurred because of the data provided by management for the quarter, the stock is not cheap enough on an absolute basis to justify a change at this time.

Danaher Shares Are Too Pricey for My Liking

For the most part, I would say that the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year was a pretty good time for shareholders of Danaher. The company reported revenue of $6.84 billion. This was up nicely from the $6.54 billion the company reported a year earlier. And it happened to be $28.4 million above what analysts were hoping to see. With this rise in revenue came higher profits as well. Earnings per share popped from $1.49 to $1.66. That was unfortunately $0.01 below what analysts were hoping to see. But it still translated to a rise in net income from $1.09 billion to $1.20 billion.

When it comes to adjusted earnings per share, the picture was better than expected. The company saw a jump from $2.14 to $2.23. That ended up being $0.04 greater than what was anticipated. And it meant a rise in adjusted net profits from about $1.56 billion to $1.61 billion. Other profitability metrics improved without exception. Take operating cash flow as an example. It increased from $2.02 billion to $2.12 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we get an increase from $1.85 billion to $1.97 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $2.12 billion to $2.13 billion. The chart above also shows results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. Although we did see net profits and operating cash flow contract, all the other metrics included in that chart showed growth year over year.

All the company's operating segments experienced growth during this time. Take, for instance, the star of the show. This was none other than its Biotechnology segment. Revenue of $2.03 billion exceeded the $1.87 billion reported the previous year. This rise of 8.8% was driven in part by foreign currency fluctuations. But even if we remove that from the equation, we would have gotten around a 6% increase in core sales growth for the year. Management continues to push sales higher through innovations. For instance, during the quarter, the company benefited from Cytiva’s launch of over 20 new products across the biologics workflow, new 500- and 2,000-liter formats of the Xcellerex X-platform bioreactor. It also launched two new Protein A resins. With this rise in sales, we also saw a nice jump in operating profit from $508 million to $540 million. But the overall margin for the company did contract slightly from 27.2% to 26.6%. That still makes it the most profitable part of the company from a margin standpoint. However, the Diagnostics segment is definitely catching up.

Year over year, it reported a rise in revenue of 3.1%, climbing from $2.64 billion to $2.72 billion. Unfortunately, core sales growth was slightly lower at 2%. The rise in sales here stemmed at least in part from the company’s Beckman Coulter Diagnostics business as it expanded its Dxl 9000 assay menu. The firm specifically singled out progress in neurodegenerative diseases such as those involving first-to-market automated, high-throughput BD-tau research-use-only immunoassay. It also continued to expand its cardiac and blood virus menus. There were other wins as well. But not all of them occurred during the quarter. About a week ago, Cepheid received FDA clearance for its Xpert GI Panel, which is a multiplex PCR test that can detect 11 common gastrointestinal pathogens using just one sample. That is hugely innovative if you ask me. While the revenue increase for this segment was modest, especially when we strip out changes in working capital, the move higher in profitability was meaningful. Segment operating profits shot up from $624 million to $713 million, with the overall operating profit margin expanding solidly from 23.7% to 26.2%.

Lastly, we have the Life Sciences segment. This part of the company saw a rise in revenue from $2.03 billion to $2.09 billion. Management continues to stress that biotechnology demand remains stable. The real contribution to the growth that the segment saw, then, involved what management described as a ‘modest’ recovery when it comes to the pharmaceutical space, with Europe being a bright spot for the business. However, in the United States and China, demand for academic and research activities is lower. And that is helping to drive down that segment’s consumables business, specifically as there is lower demand for plasmids and mRNA. Funding pressure across early-stage biotech and academic research is also problematic. It's not surprising, given these developments, that operating profits for the segment would actually contract from $376 million to $336 million.

On the whole, I am happy with what I saw here. And what's even better is the fact that management seems at least somewhat optimistic when it comes to 2026. Management expects, for instance, for core sales growth this year to be up by between 3% and 6%. This would be mostly driven by a 6% rise associated with the Biotechnology segment. As revenue grows, we should expect adjusted earnings per share to climb as well. The firm is currently targeting earnings of between $8.35 and $8.50. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net profits of about $6.04 billion. And if we scale up other profitability metrics accordingly, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $7.69 billion and EBITDA of $8.29 billion.

Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2024 and 2025, we can easily see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Honestly, these are very lofty multiples. I know that this space has a tendency to trade at rather high prices. But as a value investor, seeing a company trade in excess of 20 times cash flow is difficult to digest, especially if revenue and profit growth is only good instead of great. Of course, some investors will disagree with me. And that's fine. Their argument will likely hinge on the fact that, relative to other similar firms, the stock is actually a bit cheap. In the chart below, you can see what I mean here. On both a price-to-earnings basis and on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, Danaher is cheaper than any of the five institutions I stacked it up against. But because of the net debt picture of the business, the EV-to-EBITDA approach puts it closer to the middle of the group, with two of the firms being cheaper than it is.

Speaking of net debt, in the chart below, you can see the overall trajectory of the company when it comes to net debt and the net leverage ratio over the last few years now. You can also see how the company stands up against the same five institutions I compared it to already. With a reading of 1.80, the company actually has greater leverage than three of the five competitors. This is not awful by any means. But at the same time, I wouldn't exactly say that it is justification for some sort of premium compared to the competition. If anything, perhaps a small discount compared to those firms that have less leverage than it does would be appropriate.

Takeaway

The data available right now shows me that Danaher is doing a fine job. But as a value investor, I have to be principled. The fact of the matter is that while the stock is cheaper than most other similar firms, it is still expensive on an absolute basis. This year, the picture should look even better than it did last year. And normally, you would expect the market to respond accordingly. However, the move lower is almost certainly attributable to the fact that the market recognizes how pricey the stock is. And even with financial performance that was, for the most part, positive, pushing shares higher just did not make sense. For now, I feel comfortable with my "Hold" rating on the company. But as time goes on, I will evaluate it based on new data that's available.

data.title: AMD: Facing Its Moment Of Truth
data.description:
I first upgraded Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) to a “BUY” in Q2 2025, arguing the company would greatly benefit from a new AI hardware cycle. The stock has performed well since then, more than doubling in price.

AMD’s bull run continued through Q2 and Q3 results, despite both of these quarters being somewhat soft, with the company growing at a far slower pace than its main rival NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

With Q4, I think the time for excuses has run out. AMD needs to show their chips are gaining traction and fully benefitting from AI demand and the significant CapEx investment tied to it.

Why AMD will face anagnorisis with Q4 earnings

Anagnorisis, or in plain English “moment of recognition,” is a moment in a play when a character makes a critical discovery. I think AMD will go through such a moment during Q4 earnings on February 3rd.

The company launched its MI350 series of chips in 2025, with production only ramping up in the second half of last year. I think Q3 earnings, the last available at the time of writing, did not yet capture in full the results of MI350 chips due to production ramping up. Despite this, the company did well that quarter (recovering from a disappointing Q2) and grew revenue at its record pace of 36% year-over-year.

For Q4, I expect to see further acceleration in revenue growth driven by MI350 sales, which to me represents the core of the bullish thesis with this company.

Markets are forward looking, and with a P/E of above 60 at the time of writing, there is not a lot of room for error. In this sense, AMD has no more excuses and needs to show traction for MI350 to justify its valuation and grow further.

If things go well… CapEx spooked markets, but AMD is a beneficiary

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) just had one of its worst days on the stock market following Q4 earnings, due to an increase in CapEx and a somewhat soft growth for its cloud division. Something similar happened to Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) in November, again due to markets reacting to increased CapEx guidance for the future.

AMD, however, should be a beneficiary of this wave of AI-driven CapEx by major Tech companies. Neither Microsoft nor Meta nor any other Tech company has so far signalled they will reduce their CapEx plans. If anything, markets were spooked by the exact opposite: an increase in these plans.

In the context of Q4 earnings, I think AMD has no more excuses: since demand for chips has only grown in Q4, the company needs to show MI350 can deliver.

A bet still worth taking: who should consider buying AMD today?

I am personally very bullish on the AI race. I think it’s unlikely we are going to see a significant slow down in microchip demand. On the contrary, I recently argued that we may see an acceleration and an actual “AI bubble” forming in 2026. This is the fundamental reason why I remain bullish on AMD, even as there is a reduced margin of safety within the stock today.

My valuation model for the company, which I covered in detail in a past article, sees the company valued between $ 219 and $ 2500 + per share. The table below summarizes the assumptions behind my valuation, which is a DCF model on a 10-years time frame.

At the time of writing, AMD trades at ~$250, well above my base stock price target of $219 per share. Contrary to when I first recommended this stock, I do not think this leaves a large margin of safety to investors. If things go south in Q4, and AMD fails to convince investors about the traction of their MI350 chips, I think the company could face a significant correction (something I will cover shortly in my risk section).

However, if the AI race accelerates as I expect it to, I think AMD still has a lot of upside. Which brings me to my next point: AMD remains a compelling play for those bullish on AI, even at today’s prices. I believe semiconductors in general are a great play, as they benefit from CapEx regardless of whether AI is getting monetized immediately or not. And within semiconductors, I believe AMD is one of the best bets, a function of being the only company that today can compete directly with Nvidia, benefitting from demand that the latter cannot satisfy.

As proof of AMD being the best bet in semiconductors today, consider the company outperformed even the Direxion Daily Semiconductor Bull 3X Shares ETF (SOXL) in the last five years. This is a 3X leveraged ETF (!) that is exposed to AMD itself to the tune of ~5% at the time of writing. I recommended SOXL as a high-risk bet on AI during the correction in late Q3 last year. I expect AMD to keep beating its peers (even if not necessarily SOXL) for the foreseeable future.

If things do not go well... (risks to my bullish thesis)

At the cost of repeating myself, I will say once again that a bet on AMD today is a bet on MI350 competing with Nvidia’s chips in efficiency and AI applications, especially those related to inference.

Readers may notice that I chose not even to mention AMD’s next generation of chips, the MI450 Series. These are set to be released later in 2026 and will be crucial for the future of the company. However, my eyes are still on the MI350 story: if AMD fails to show traction in Q4, then the stock may correct deeply, and only rebound if markets are ever convinced AMD can crack its next generation of chips.

On top of AMD facing its “moment of truth” in Q4, other risks include the company being exposed to AI, the current direction of interest rates (higher rates would negatively affect CapEx in data centers) and, more generally, stock volatility.

Anyone thinking to enter AMD today should carefully consider the fact that any margin of safety is likely gone, and the stock already prices in a successful AI semiconductor player.

Conclusion: I remain bullish as we enter Agentic AI and Robotics

In much of my past work I described the idea that the AI race is unfolding in four phases:

  1. A “Picks & Shovels” phase, where GPU companies like Nvidia and AMD supply chips for all types of AI applications.

  2. A “Cloud & Early Adopters” phase where giants such as Microsoft and Meta integrate AI for efficiency in their business (improving the ROI of ads or automating their workforce) while scaling cloud platforms.

  3. An Agentic AI phase, where more applications will emerge, automating workflows in software development, sales, and finance.

  4. A Robotics phase where AI meets the real world in autonomy and humanoids—something I discussed in my latest article about Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

I think 2026 is the year of Agentic AI, with AI applications starting to have an impact on businesses (and potentially impacting the job market and starting to reshape the economy). In this context, AMD will need to prove they have what it takes to “attend the party” with their chips. The time of excuses has run out, and I see Q4 earnings as the moment of truth. I personally remain bullish on the company and its ability to capture AI demand, and reiterate my BUY rating, with a long-term view.

data.title: Fidelity Contrafund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the fourth quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 2.61%, versus 2.66% for the benchmark, the S&P 500® index. Stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace, supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. The past three months, we continued to favor fast-growing, "best of breed" firms. Importantly, given our focus, longer-term performance comparisons strongly favor the fund over the benchmark.

Stocks entered October on an uptrend, but with the federal government in a shutdown that would last until November 12. Equities rose nonetheless, boosted by earnings momentum and a constructive expansionary backdrop. On October 29, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points at its second consecutive meeting, as it looked to balance a recent slowdown in hiring and inflation running higher than its 2% target. The S&P 500® rose 2.34% for the month.

Momentum faded a bit in November (+0.25%), as stocks weathered the shutdown and a brief mid-month sell-off that mostly reflected concern about an AI bubble. However, the focus returned to a U.S. economy largely driven by massive AI-related outlays by big tech. Meanwhile, the Fed cut rates by another quarter point on December 10. The index achieved a record close on Christmas Eve before a sluggish final week resulted in a flattish gain for the month, a 17.88% rise for the year and a 38.65% increase since the low on April 8.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented health care sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI, as media & entertainment stocks were up 9%.

All other groups lagged the index for the three months. Real estate (-3%) and utilities (-1%), small components of the S&P 500, each lost ground, while consumer staples finished at roughly breakeven. Materials, industrials and consumer discretionary stocks all gained roughly 1%, as did information technology, which represented 35% of the index in Q4. Financials (+2%) reflected a strong showing by banks (+7%) but weakness (-1%) among insurers, and energy (+2%) was held back by a sharp decline in the price of oil.

Security selection in the capital goods industry within the industrials sector notably contributed to the fund's performance versus the S&P 500® in Q4. This mostly reflected a sizable non-benchmark stake in Space Exploration Technologies (+89%), a private company that makes spacecraft and provides satellite communications and launch capabilities. We like SpaceX, in part, for its Starlink business focusing on low-earth-orbit satellites, which has created a web of broadband internet access to previously underserved communities in rural and remote areas. In December, investors applauded reports of the potential for an initial public offering in 2026.

In tech, largely avoiding Oracle (ORCL) contributed to relative performance. In Q4, shares of the software and cloud-services provider returned about -31%, so we made a good decision in eliminating the fund's scant exposure in November. We were skeptical about the profitability of Oracle's cloud business relative to peers, including Amazon.com (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT).

Conversely, our stock picks in media & entertainment stood out as the largest detractor versus the benchmark, especially overweights in Meta Platforms (META) (-10%), parent of Facebook and Instagram, and video-streaming service provider Netflix (NFLX) (-22%).

In October, Meta reported record quarterly revenue but net income that was much lower than expected, largely due to spending on AI infrastructure. The company said it expects total expenses to "grow at a significantly faster percentage rate in 2026 than 2025." We like that Meta has embraced AI to improve the quality and relevance of the content and advertisements it shows its users. We reduced exposure to Meta the past three months, but the stock remained by far the top holding and overweight at year-end.

Shares of Netflix fell due to weaker-than-expected Q3 earnings and an announced plan to buy Warner Bros. Q3 revenue growth was in line with guidance, but operating margin missed due to a tax dispute with Brazil that cut into earnings. The stock fell further in December after Netflix announced the proposed Warner Bros. purchase, an acquisition that would include Warner's film and television studios, HBO Max and HBO. Investors worried the deal could face regulatory scrutiny. We lowered the fund's stake in Netflix in Q4, but it remained a large holding and overweight, based on its leadership position and the stickiness of its business.

Outlook and Positioning

As 2026 begins, the global and U.S. business cycles remain constructive, with monetary and fiscal easing in the U.S. likely to continue, in our view. Noteworthy trends in the U.S. include strong corporate fundamentals and earnings momentum, softening labor markets, and wealth-powered high-end consumption. Capital spending by corporations has experienced a powerful AI-driven upswing, although signs of broadening activity remain faint. We believe the massive tax-and-spending package that became law on July 4 provides an additional tailwind for corporate earnings growth.

The Fed cut its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25% percentage points in September, October and December, amid signs of softer employment conditions. The market and Fed expect two more rate cuts in 2026, due to expectations for soft labor markets and short-lived inflation. By December, central banks in the eurozone, the U.K. and Canada were all on pause.

The near-term outlook appears favorable for continued economic and business expansion, but we believe policy uncertainty, inflation persistence and elevated asset valuations bear watching. Uncertainty related to economic policy dropped in the second half of 2025 after hitting a record high in April, but it has remained elevated and stands in sharp contrast to stock-market volatility, which declined back to roughly its historical average.

The U.S. dollar dropped roughly 10% against major currencies in 2025, its worst calendar year since 2017. Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yields finished 2025 effectively unchanged at around 4.2%, completing a fairly range-bound year.

After two years of near-zero goods inflation, tariff hikes led to a rise in goods prices in the second half of 2025. Inflation remained sticky in housing and services, keeping headline consumer inflation elevated and notably higher than the Fed's 2% target.

In managing the fund, we follow the investing tenet that a stock's price follows the actual and expected earnings per share of the underlying company over time. So, as the earnings outlook for the fund's holdings changed in Q4, we made only modest adjustments.

Combining technology and communication services, the fund is overweight what we consider tech-related "growth plays." The embrace of AI by companies, governments and individuals is the biggest trend in the market, in our view. We believe AI will affect every business, so we are working closely with the Fidelity research team to identify winners and losers in this changing tech landscape. Accordingly, the fund's position in communication services, including sizable commitments to Meta, Google parent Alphabet (GOOGL) and Netflix, represented 22% of assets and was by far the top sector overweight. Meta is classified as a media name, but we view it as a leading, founder-led tech firm that has embraced AI to improve two critical parts of its business: choosing content and ads to show users, and helping advertisers create better ads.

Tech represented 26% of assets at year-end, the top sector allocation and essentially unchanged from September 30. The biggest holdings here are AI-focused chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA), software and cloud giant Microsoft, personal-electronics giant Apple, and Amphenol, which makes electronic and fiber-optic connectors.

The fund's allocation to the financials sector was about 16%, a bit lower than three months ago but still a meaningful overweight. Insurance-focused conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) anchors the fund's position in the sector, followed by much smaller investments in banking firm JPMorgan Chase and credit-card processor Visa.

We continue to seek more exposure to international stocks and small- and mid-cap firms. The latter are often new issues from the past five to seven years. We believe many of these businesses, which we call "tomorrow's blue chips," can grow faster than the largest companies in the market.

Fidelity® Contrafund® (FCNTX)

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Contrafund® is an opportunistic, diversified equity strategy with a large-cap growth bias.

Philosophically, we believe stock prices follow companies' earnings, and those companies that can deliver durable multiyear earnings growth provide attractive investment opportunities.

As a result, our investment approach seeks firms we believe are poised for sustained, above-average earnings growth that is not accurately reflected in the stocks' current valuation.

In particular, we emphasize companies with "best of breed" qualities, including those with a strong competitive position, high returns on capital, solid free-cash-flow generation and management teams that are stewards of shareholder capital.

We strive to uncover these investment opportunities through in-depth bottom-up, fundamental analysis, working in concert with Fidelity's global research team.
